tabs.name: Home
tabs.intro: The threshold where appearance and presence stop costing you confidence.

blocks.x: The point where you're put-together enough to stop being self-conscious — so your attention is free to be present with the person in front of you.

Becoming the fullest version of yourself takes years — physique, dance, swimming, social mastery, taste. That's the long road, and it's worth walking. But you don't get to wait for the finished version before you live. This defines the minimum you need to feel ready enough to put yourself out there now: ask someone out, get good photos, get on Hinge, walk into a room and talk to people.
An MVP grants readiness, not confidence

An MVP isn't a set of things that grant confidence. It's the point where you're put-together enough to stop being self-conscious, so your attention is free to actually be present. Hit the minimum, then go live — everything deeper gets built while you're already out there.

args.caption: Cross the inner threshold, then keep building on the outside.

blocks.cap: You don't wait for the outer ring; you cross the MVP line and start living.

blocks.x: How to read this library

Each category is a tab; the left panel moves between its parts. Parts that matter carry a Status (Active now / Action item / Completed / Excluded) and a Priority (High / Low) you can tap to change — they save on this device. The On Work tab is the running to-do: anything Active or Action item shows there, split by priority.
The nine categories

items.x: Your live to-do — what's Active and what's an Action item.
Highest return-per-effort — mostly habits, not money.
One of your three real gaps. A system, not a wardrobe.
Your third real gap — a tactical weekend project.
The engine that creates the encounters.
Clothes fit, you don't feel weak. Mostly there.
The highest-leverage area of all.
Culture + interests: conversation fuel and venues.

blocks.x: The rule: clear the action items and you've passed the bar to go out — the rest improves in motion. And plainly: being early to dating is completely normal. This exists to get you out sooner, not to certify you as ready.

tabs.name: Grooming
tabs.intro: The highest return-per-effort category — mostly habits, not money.

blocks.x: Two parts. Skin is a solved habit. Facial hair is the open decision — and for a round face it's one of your best structure tools.

items.x: Cleanser, moisturizer, sunscreen, lip balm. Never oily, dry, or flaky.
Consciously pick a style instead of letting it default.

blocks.x: Skin — the minimum, handled
Clean, consistent skin is a habit, not a product hunt. You already have this handled — protect it, don't over-engineer it.

items: Cleanser, moisturizer, sunscreen — morning and night. Sunscreen is the single highest-return step.
A lip balm so lips never crack.
Minimum: never oily, dry, or flaky. You've got this.

blocks.x: Facial hair — pick a style on purpose
You've never consciously picked a style — the move is to decide one instead of letting it default. For a round face, facial hair is the cheapest way to add angles and vertical length.

items.x: Sharpest look, but exposes a round face the most. Highest bar to pull off.
Adds jaw definition, lowest effort — the safest place to start.
The most face-shaping option; adds the most length and angle.

args.title: Round face: length toward the chin adds structure

blocks.cap: Stubble is the low-risk default; a chin-forward short beard shapes the most.

blocks.x: Shaping rules for a round face

items: Keep cheeks and sides tidy and a touch shorter, with more length toward the chin — adds vertical length and angles.
Avoid chin-straps and round shapes that echo the face.
Pairs with the haircut: stubble or a short beard is exactly what balances a buzz or short crop (see Hair).

blocks.x: How to decide — the 80/20

steps: Grow 1–2 weeks to see real density and patchiness
Barber shapes it — cheek + neck lines set once, properly
Re-trim weekly — neck and cheek lines only, keep it sharp

blocks.cap: Designer stubble sits in the sweet spot: low effort, low risk, real definition.

blocks.x: Decide it this way

80/20: start with designer stubble — lowest effort, adds jaw definition, near-zero risk. If you want more shaping, grow to a short chin-forward beard. Only go clean-shaven once the rest of the look is dialed in.

topics.name: Hair
topics.blurb: First principles → every style → your cut.

blocks.x: See the head as a set of zones and a haircut as four adjustable levers. Then you stop copying photos blindly and start choosing on purpose — and you can tell any barber exactly what you want.
First principle — what a haircut actually is

A haircut is not a shape you pick off a poster. It is a set of decisions made zone by zone across your head: how much length to leave, how much weight (bulk) to remove, what silhouette that creates, and how the ends are finished. Every named style — buzz, fade, mullet, quiff — is just a preset of those four decisions applied to the zones. Learn the levers and the zones and you can read, request, or invent any cut.
The four levers (every choice is one of these)

items: Length — how long the hair is in a zone. Sets the ceiling on what's possible: you can always take away, never add on the day.
Weight / bulk — how dense and heavy the hair is. Removing weight (thinning, texturising, point-cutting) makes hair lighter and look fuller without losing length.
Shape / silhouette — the outline from front and side: where volume sits (up, forward, back, flat) and how sides taper into the top.
Texture / finish — how the ends are cut (blunt = sharp, point-cut = soft) plus product. Decides sharp-vs-messy and matte-vs-glossy.

blocks.cap: Any named style is these four levers, set differently across the zones of the head.

blocks.x: The map — the zones of the head

Barbers think in zones, so learn the words. Once you can name where you want a change, instructions stop being vague (“not too short”) and become precise (“leave the fringe, tighten the sides, texture the top”). Drag or scroll the diagram to explore it.

blocks.cap: Head in profile — face on the left. Orange marks the temple corners, where recession usually starts.

head: Zone
Where it is
What the cut controls here
rows: The front section that falls onto the forehead
Coverage of the forehead/hairline; the first thing people see
The outline where hair meets skin at the front
How sharp or soft the front reads; boxed vs natural corners
The two front corners above the temples
The classic recession spot; face framing; the ‘M’ shape
Contrast with the top; where fades and tapers live
The flat crown-facing area on top
The main length you style; height and forward/back flow
The swirl / spiral at the back-top
Cowlicks and how hair sits; common thinning spot
The rounded occipital area behind the crown
Weight and how the shape rounds off
The hairline at the back of the neck
How clean the finish looks; blocked, rounded, or tapered

blocks.x: Region by region — what gets changed and why
The same length means different things in different zones. Here is what a barber is actually adjusting in each, and the trade-off to know.

items: Fringe — kept long it covers the forehead and receding corners; cut short and forward it becomes a ‘crop’ fringe. Heavy fringes look flat; texturised ones look fuller. Your main tool for coverage.
Hairline / edge — leaving it natural (soft) suits receding or uneven hairlines; ‘boxing’ it in (sharp) suits strong, full hairlines. Forcing a sharp line onto a weak hairline exposes the gaps.
Temples / corners — you can’t grow these back with scissors, so the choice is frame them (leave fringe length to cover) or own them (go shorter so there’s no line to defend).
Sides — the contrast lever. Short sides push attention to the top and make it look fuller; long sides add width (good for narrow faces, bad for round ones).
Top — where length and height live. More height elongates a round/short face; flat-and-forward suits long faces. Weight removed here stops the top collapsing.
Crown — cowlicks and the swirl decide which way hair naturally falls; a good barber cuts with it, not against it. Fighting the crown gives a piece that never sits.
Back — mostly rounding the shape cleanly and removing bulk so the head doesn’t look boxy from behind.
Nape — a blocked (straight) nape reads formal, a rounded nape softer, and a tapered (faded-out) nape lasts longest between cuts.

blocks.x: Hair type — the hand you’re dealt

Two independent axes decide what a style will actually do: texture (how straight or curly) and strand + density (how fine or coarse each hair is, and how many you have). A cut that looks great on thick straight hair can fail completely on fine wavy hair.

args.title: Texture axis: straighter hair shows the cut’s shape; curlier hair shows volume

blocks.cap: Texture is one axis. Thickness (fine ↔ coarse) and density (sparse ↔ dense) are separate ones.

head: Hair type
Strength
Watch-out
Tends to suit
rows: Sharp, clean lines; shows a precise cut
Shows scalp when thin; can look flat
Crops, fades, side parts, slick styles
Can get shapeless if too long
Shrinks when dry; needs its own routine
Shorter lengths, blunt-ish crops, matte product
Lots to work with; holds shape
Gets heavy and ‘puffy’; needs weight removed
Most styles — but ask to texturise/thin

blocks.x: Face shape — the target is always balance

The whole game of choosing a cut is nudging your face toward a balanced oval. If a face is round, add height and reduce width; if it’s long, add width and avoid height; if it’s square, soften the corners. Use the hair to add what’s missing and calm what’s excessive.

args.caption: Every choice nudges the shape toward the centre.

blocks.cap: The goal isn’t a specific style — it’s balance. The cut is how you get there.

head: Face shape
Goal
Add
Avoid
rows: Covering the face until it reads round
Height on top, tight sides, some angle
Width at the sides, floppy fringe, mushroom shapes
Texture, a little length, softer edges
Ultra-hard lines that echo the jaw
Width via fuller sides, a fringe
Big height on top (makes it longer)
Volume piled high at the front
Tight sides that narrow it further

blocks.x: The famous hairstyles — tap a family to open it
Grouped by how much length they keep. Click a family below to expand the full list, what defines each cut (in zones), who it suits, and how often to re-cut.

items.x: Buzz · crew · Caesar · crop — lowest upkeep, strongest for thick or thinning hair

blocks.x: Length is short everywhere, so the shape of your head and hairline do the talking. These are the most honest and confident options if you’re receding — there’s little to hide.

head: Style
What defines it (zones/levers)
Best for
Re-cut
rows: One guard length all over; no fringe
Thick or thinning hair; strong head shape
Short, a touch longer at the front top; tidy tapered sides
Short top combed forward into a short straight fringe
Short textured top + short forward fringe + faded sides
Fine or receding hair; round/long faces

blocks.x: Round face tip: pair any of these with defined stubble or a short beard to add jaw angles, and ask for a little height at the front so it isn’t flat.

items.x: Fades · undercut · pompadour · quiff · comb-over · slick back — the modern default

blocks.x: The defining move is contrast: sides taken down with a fade or taper while the top keeps length to style. Where the short-to-long line sits, and whether it’s blended or a hard step, changes everything.

args.title: Fade height = how far up the short part climbs

blocks.cap: Lower fade = subtle, grows out well. Higher fade = more contrast, more upkeep.

head: Style
What defines it (zones/levers)
Best for
Re-cut
rows: Sides blended from skin upward; height = how far it climbs
Adds contrast; makes the top look fuller
A gentle fade only around ears + nape
Long top, short sides, NO blend — a hard step
Top swept up & back with height at the front
Thick hair; adds height (great for round faces)
Front lifted up & slightly forward; softer than a pomp
Top combed to one side over a part; faded sides
All top length combed straight back with product
Full hair only — reveals a weak hairline

blocks.x: Thinning-hair tip: keep the fade a taper (not skin) and the top textured, not slicked. High contrast + shine is what exposes a sparse top.

items.x: Flow · curtains · mullet · man-bun — commitment and the right hair type

blocks.x: Length becomes the whole look. These need decent density and usually wave or thickness to hold shape — on fine or receding hair they tend to expose more than they cover.

head: Style
What defines it (zones/levers)
Best for
Re-cut
rows: Grown-out top & sides that flow back / over the ears
Medium top parted in the middle, framing the face
Short/faded sides, length kept at the back (modern = textured, not flicked)
Long top tied up; sides long or faded
Long thick hair; commitment to grow

blocks.x: Reality check: growing it out is the most common mistake on a receding hairline — weight makes hair separate and show scalp. If you go long, keep the weight out and the fringe textured.
How to actually think about a cut (the method)

blocks.cap: The loop: read what you’ve got, aim for balance, choose a preset, translate to zones, maintain.

blocks.x: The one idea to keep: you are not choosing a picture, you are choosing length + weight + shape + finish for each zone, aimed at balancing your face and flattering the hair you have. A photo is just shorthand for those settings.
How to ask the barber (speak in zones + levers)
Bring 2–3 reference photos (including one of your hair wet and pushed back so they see your real hairline), then describe it in zones so nothing is left to guessing.

steps: Name the preset: e.g. “textured crop” or “mid taper”.
Sides: how short and how high (“low/mid/high fade” or “just a taper, nothing too tight”).
Top: length + “take the weight out / texturise” rather than only “shorter”.
Fringe & corners: how much to keep for coverage or framing.
Nape: blocked, rounded, or tapered out.
Finish: matte for fuller-looking hair; save shine for full, thick hair.

blocks.x: Your situation — recession + round face
Advanced recession + round face. The principle: work with the hairline, not against it. Owning it beats concealing it — every time.
The principle

With advanced recession, length needs hairline density to frame the face — which is exactly what's gone — so length draws the eye straight to the recession. The winning move is a cut designed around the hairline you actually have.

blocks.cap: Two paths: fighting the hairline (left) vs working with it (right).

blocks.x: Option 1 — Textured crop with a forward fringe

Your best 'keeps some length' choice. Short-to-medium top worn deliberately forward as a textured fringe, with tight high-faded sides. The fringe sits over the receded corners and camouflages them naturally — the legit version of a comb-forward.

items: For a round face, ask for height / texture + a high fade to add vertical lines and angles.
Products: a matte clay or paste + a sea-salt spray for texture. Never gel or anything shiny — it separates hair and shows scalp.

blocks.x: Option 2 — Buzz / short crop + facial hair
Sharpest, lowest-maintenance. On advanced recession this usually looks the most confident, and there's nothing to hide.

items: Round-face fix: pair it with defined stubble or a short beard to add jaw angles.
Near-zero upkeep. Product: nothing but sunscreen on the scalp. Worth trying once before dismissing.

blocks.x: Option 3 — Keeping real length on top
Not recommended. Possible, but needs constant management and highlights exactly what you're trying to downplay. This is the one to talk yourself out of.
Decision guide

blocks.cap: Two live options (crop, buzz) and one to avoid (length).

blocks.x: What to ask the barber

steps: Bring 2–3 reference photos
Say 'textured crop with a fringe' or 'short crop', plus 'high skin/taper fade'
Add 'cut to work with a receding hairline'
Re-cut every 2–4 weeks to keep it sharp

blocks.x: Regrowth / medical (optional — no pressure)

Since this is advanced male-pattern recession, if keeping or regaining hair genuinely matters to you, the full menu includes dermatologist-prescribed minoxidil and finasteride (can slow and partly reverse loss) and hair transplants. These are medical decisions with real trade-offs — see a dermatologist or trichologist rather than self-prescribing. Plenty of men look great just owning the cut, so treat this as information, not a must-do.
Decide it this way

80/20: if you want minimum fuss and maximum confidence, buzz / short crop + defined stubble. If you want to keep some length, textured crop with a forward fringe + high fade. Either beats fighting the hairline.
Anatomy of a frame — how to read any pair

Stop seeing a frame as one object. It's three zones — front, bridge, temples — plus a set of measurements. Describe each by shape, size, material, thickness and finish and you can specify (and picture) any pair in words.

blocks.cap: The parts. Front = rim/eyewire around each lens; bridge over the nose; nose pads; end piece → hinge → temple (the arm); temple tip over the ear.
The spec code printed inside a temple: lens width ▢ bridge – temple length, in mm. Plus lens height and total frame width.

blocks.x: Shape — just axis one

blocks.cap: The common shapes. For a longer face, angular/rectangular shapes add structure; round softens.

blocks.x: Rim, bridge & temple styles

blocks.cap: Rim style — how much surrounds the lens: full-rim, semi-rimless (top only), rimless.
Bridge type — how it sits on the nose. Metal frames add adjustable nose pads; acetate uses a moulded bridge.
Temple (arm) style — how it grips: skull curves behind the ear, library is straight, cable wraps around.

blocks.x: Describe any frame with 8 axes: shape · rim style · material (acetate / metal / TR90) · thickness · colour & finish · bridge type & fit · temple style · proportion. Everything below is about picking yours.
How a face maps to a frame — the logic
The whole thing runs on one idea: the face states a problem, the frame is the correction. Four rules govern every choice, and each face trait points to exactly one frame lever.

blocks.cap: The four governing rules. Contrast the shape; harmonise the colour; match the scale; treat the frame as jewellery for the eyes.

blocks.x: The mapping — every face trait points to a frame lever

blocks.cap: Read left → right: each property of the face sets one decision about the frame.

blocks.x: Shape — contrast the face

blocks.cap: Pick a frame shape that opposes the face's dominant lines — that's what neutralises them.

blocks.x: Colour — harmonise with your colouring

blocks.cap: Undertone sets the hue family; depth of colouring sets how dark you go; personal contrast sets how bold.

blocks.x: The order you decide in

blocks.cap: Fit is mechanics and comes first; shape, scale, position and colour follow; the statement is last.

blocks.x: Your readout

items.x: Rectangular / soft-geometric — angular enough to add structure, rounded enough not to over-harden a lean face. Wider than tall.
Medium-to-bold acetate — you can carry structure; thin wire disappears on you.
Top rim on your strong brow; keep the top medium-toned, not heavy-dark, so your deep-set eyes stay forward.
Longish nose → a keyhole or higher bridge shortens it; adjustable pads if it slides.
Warm / olive undertone → tortoise, warm brown, honey, olive, gunmetal, warm navy — over cool black/silver.
Pick one: a rich tortoise (colour) OR a bold matte (thickness) — never both.

blocks.x: Net: a soft-angular, medium-bold, warm-toned rectangular/geometric frame — wider than tall, top rim on the brow. That's your lane. Fit first, then this.

Glasses aren't grooming exactly, but they sit on your face and get read before anything else — so we treat them here. For you this is the single highest-impact appearance fix. This page goes deep: terminology, frame shapes, materials, colour, fit rules, and a face-shape decision framework.

Your face is longer than wide (oblong / oval). The whole game: add width and break the length — angular shapes, a strong horizontal line, decorative temples — without over-sharpening an already lean face.
Terminology — the vocabulary of a frame
You can't choose well without the words. Here's the 20% of terminology that drives 80% of good decisions.

head: Term
What it means
Why it matters
rows: Distance across your cheekbones/temples — the widest part of your face
Frames should not be wider than this
Outer edge to outer edge of the two lenses + bridge
Should roughly match your face width, never exceed it
Width of one lens, in mm
Larger = bolder; eyes should sit centred in it
The piece over the nose joining the two lenses
Sets how frames sit; too wide = they slide down
The two sides running to your ears
Where the fit/level comes from; where logos live
Length of the arms, in mm
The upper rim of the frame
Should sit near — and roughly follow — your brows
Full / semi / none defines how bold the frame reads

blocks.cap: Anatomy of a frame: face width sets the ceiling; everything else fits inside it.

blocks.x: How face shape relates to frames: a good frame contrasts the face shape. A longer-than-wide (oblong/oval) face wants frames that add width and cut the length — a strong horizontal line, decorative or contrasting temples, and enough lens depth to fill the vertical. Soft-angular shapes add structure without over-hardening a lean face; pure curves just lengthen it further.
Frame shapes — names, why, and traditional vs modern
Frame families are named for their silhouette or their origin. Knowing the name gets you the right frame in any shop or search.

blocks.cap: The frame-family tree: angular, rounded, iconic, and by rim style.

head: Shape
Why it's called that
Read
rows: Lenses wider than tall — a rectangle
Classic, structured. Adds width + straight lines — great for round faces
Soft, intellectual, retro. Echoes a round face — avoid
Gentle, safe on most faces; too soft for a round face
Designed for WWII pilots ('aviators') — teardrop lenses, double bridge
Cool, classic. Angular teardrop adds some structure
Heavy top bar mimics the brow line
Sharp, intellectual, retro-modern. Adds a strong horizontal line up top
Upswept outer corners like a cat's eye
Upward lift, more traditionally feminine but unisex versions exist
Named for the polygon shape (6+ sides)
Modern, distinctive. All straight lines — excellent for round faces
Thin round metal, worn by John Lennon
Vintage, artsy. Round — avoid for a round face
No rim, or rim only on top
Minimal, invisible, professional. Soft — less face-shaping

blocks.cap: Where the families sit on the traditional-vs-modern axis.

blocks.x: Materials

items.x: Thick plastic (from cotton/wood). Bold, colourful, warm — bigger visual weight. Where tortoise & statement frames live.
Thin, minimal, light. Reads understated and professional. Gunmetal and silver-tones.
Premium metal: ultra-light, strong, hypoallergenic. Minimal look, higher price.
Flexible nylon polymer. Light, near-unbreakable, sporty/casual. Great everyday durability.

head: Rim style
Look
Best for
rows: Adding definition to a soft/round face

blocks.x: Colour — versatile picks and how colour changes the read
Colour changes how bold a frame reads and how it plays against your skin and hair. Start with a versatile neutral; add character later.

items.x: Warm brown mottled pattern. Adds character while staying neutral — pairs with almost everything.
Softer than black, warm, flattering on most skin tones. Safe and versatile.
Dark grey metal. Minimal, modern, understated — disappears into any outfit.
Bold and clean without the shine of gloss black. The safest 'statement' neutral.

items: Darker, thicker, bolder colours = more of a statement and more face structure.
Thin metal / neutral tones = the frame recedes and reads minimal.
Because your Fashion rule is 'one statement per outfit', if the frame is your statement, keep the rest quiet.

blocks.x: Fit rules — the non-negotiables

blocks.cap: The four fit rules that decide whether any frame works.

items: Frames should not be wider than the widest part of your face.
The top edge sits near your brows and roughly follows them.
Your eyes are roughly centred in each lens.
They sit level and don't slide down your nose.

blocks.x: Decision framework — face shape → frames

The master rule: frame shape should contrast face shape. Your face is longer than wide (oblong/oval), so you want angular structure that adds width and breaks the vertical — soft-angular, not razor-sharp; a horizontal line and decorative temples help.

head: Face shape
Go for
Avoid
rows: Rectangular, square, geometric/hexagonal, browline — straight lines add structure & length
Round, oval, round-metal — they echo and soften

items: Round
Oval
Round-metal
→ avoid for you

items: Rectangular
Geometric
Square
→ your zone

bl.title: Soft + minimal
items: Rimless
Oval-metal

br.title: Angular + bold
items: Browline
Thick rectangular
Most structure

blocks.cap: Your zone is the right side — angular. The more structure, the more it fixes a round face.

steps: Round face? → angular / rectangular / geometric
Add structure → straight top edge, some width, matte or dark colour
Check the 4 fit rules → not wider than face, brow line, centred eyes, sit level
Pick a versatile colour → tortoise / dark brown / gunmetal / matte black
Try many in person → take a friend or photos to the optician

blocks.x: Practical

items: Get an anti-glare coating and keep the lenses smudge-free always.
Contacts or a second frame later add variety once the main pair is right.

blocks.x: Decide it this way

80/20 for you: one well-fitting angular / rectangular or geometric full-rim pair, in tortoise, dark brown, gunmetal, or matte black, that passes the four fit rules. Take a friend (or photos) to the optician and try a lot of them — the right pair is obvious once it's on your face.
Small surface area, outsized signal. People clock hands more than you'd think — this is a solved habit for you, so the job is just not letting it slip.

items: Nails trimmed short and clean — no dirt underneath.
Moisturize hands so they're not dry or cracked.
Minimum: nothing on your hands that makes someone hesitate. (Handled.)

blocks.x: Decide it this way
80/20: a weekly trim + hand cream. That's the whole job. Marked completed — protect it, don't overthink it.
Fresh breath is a hard floor for any close interaction — non-negotiable, and already handled.

items: Brush twice daily, floss, scrape the tongue.
Carry gum or mints by default before any social setting.
Whitening is a nice extra later; clean and fresh is the minimum now. (Handled.)

blocks.x: Decide it this way
80/20: the daily routine + mints in your pocket clears 100% of the risk. Whitening is optional polish, not part of the MVP.

topics.name: Face Analysis
topics.blurb: A full read of your face — in diagrams.

blocks.x: Everything from the deep-dive, turned into diagrams. This maps three things: the structure (what's fixed and already working for you), the skin (what's changeable), and the signal (what your face communicates at rest). It's a map to give the other grooming tabs a reason — not a verdict.

items.x: Lean, angular, longer-than-wide. Good cheekbones and jaw. This is the foundation and it's a good one.
Combination-oily with post-acne marks and under-eye shadow. All of it is the changeable part.
Reads intense at rest; the eyes and a half-smile soften it instantly.

blocks.x: 01 · Structure — shape & proportions

blocks.cap: Oblong / rectangular leaning oval: length clearly greater than width, parallel sides, gentle taper to a slightly narrow chin.

blocks.x: Your face is clearly longer than it is wide, with fairly parallel sides. Length-dominant faces look most balanced when styling adds a little width and breaks the vertical — which is exactly why the Hair and Glasses tabs push structure and horizontal lines.
02 · Anatomy — feature map

blocks.cap: Broad forehead, heavy straight brows over deep-set hooded eyes, a long straight nose, balanced medium lips, cheekbones that read, and a narrow defined chin.

blocks.x: The brow-plus-deep-set-eyes combination is what gives your resting face its intensity (see diagram 07). Everything here is well-proportioned — nothing fighting anything else.
03 · Skin — how it behaves, and what's on it

blocks.cap: Combination leaning oily: the T — forehead, nose, chin — carries most of the shine; cheeks catch some too.
The changeable stuff: under-eye darkness, post-acne marks (discolouration, not pitted scars — these fade), a few active breakouts, and mild texture on the cheeks.

items: Oiliness: a lightweight moisturiser (not heavy) plus daily sunscreen actually reduces oil over time — skipping moisturiser makes oily skin overproduce.
The brown marks: the single biggest lever is daily sunscreen — sun makes post-acne marks linger far longer.
Under-eye: partly structural (deep-set eyes, little fat padding), partly sleep, hydration and screen time.
Lips: read slightly dehydrated — a balm is a five-second fix.

blocks.x: 04 · Bone structure — your strong angle

blocks.cap: Little facial fat means the structure shows cleanly: defined cheekbones, a clean jaw angle, a lean taper to the chin. The faint nose bump reads as character, not flaw.

blocks.x: The three-quarter angle is genuinely your most flattering — worth remembering for photos (see Photos / Socials → Posing).
05 · Colouring — tone & undertone

blocks.cap: A warm, golden-to-olive undertone at a medium-deep depth.

blocks.x: This is the same palette logic the Fashion tab uses — olive, sage, beige, rust and warm navy all sit beautifully against your skin; icy pastels and cool greys next to the face are trickier.
06 · The signal — what your face says at rest

blocks.cap: At rest you read serious and a little intense — driven by the heavy brow, deep-set eyes, and simply not smiling. It swings warm the moment the eyes engage.

blocks.x: Worth knowing because in photos a neutral face like yours can look sterner than you feel. The fix isn't structural — it's engaging the eyes and a slight smile.

The honest summary: strong, lean bone structure with good cheekbones and jaw — a foundation you already have. Thick hair, a characterful nose, balanced lips. The things that are changeable and move the needle most: managing the oiliness and post-acne marks, the under-eye darkness, and keeping skin and lips hydrated. Structure is the gift; the rest is maintenance.

A note: this is a friendly, practical read to guide grooming choices — not a clinical assessment. For the skin points, a dermatologist can tailor a real routine. Treat it as a starting map, not a scorecard.

tabs.name: Fashion
tabs.intro: A system, not a wardrobe: solid basics, a few formulas, a handful of accessories.

blocks.x: One of your three real gaps. You don't need a wardrobe — you need a system: solid basics on top and bottom, a few outfit formulas, and a handful of accessories.

items.x: A cheap tee that fits perfectly beats an expensive one that doesn't. Shoulders, chest, length — get those right and you're 80% there.
Build neutral, well-fitting basics, then add ONE statement per outfit. Two compete.
3–4 formulas you trust. Decision-free dressing is what confidence actually feels like.

blocks.x: Outfit = Fitted Basics × 2–3  +  ONE statement  ·  Neutral + 1 colour

blocks.cap: Fit is the foundation; basics are the canvas; one statement is the paint.

blocks.x: 80/20: get the fit right and keep the palette tight. That alone puts you ahead of most.
A few plain, perfect-fitting tops do most of the work.

items: 3–4 plain tees that fit at the shoulder and hit mid-fly in length: white, black, grey, navy.
1 casual shirt (oxford or linen) for a step up from a tee.
1 layer: an overshirt, bomber, or clean jacket — instantly elevates any tee.
Minimum: everything sits clean on the shoulders. Tailor anything boxy — cheap and transformative.

blocks.x: Two or three bottoms that go with everything up top.

items: 1 pair dark slim/straight jeans — the workhorse, pairs with every top.
1 pair chinos in a neutral (beige, olive, or navy).
Optional: one black trouser for smarter settings.
Minimum: correct length (slight or no break at the shoe) and a fit that isn't baggy or painted-on.

blocks.x: Your vibe: minimal base + one current element. Built for heat. Clean & minimal is the foundation; 'trendy' is one modern touch per outfit — never two.
The three formulas

items.x: Heavyweight plain tee (white/black/sage) + relaxed straight-leg trousers or dark jeans + clean minimal sneakers. Current element: the relaxed cut, or a cap.
Plain tee + open camp-collar (Cuban) short-sleeve shirt over it + light trousers + clean shoes. Current element: the shirt, or a thin silver chain.
Crisp linen/cotton shirt untucked, sleeves rolled + tailored chinos + minimal leather sneakers or loafers. Current element: a watch, or the shirt colour.

args.caption: Minimal core, exactly one current element.

blocks.cap: Minimalism is the current look — one modern touch gives 'fashion-forward' at almost no risk.

blocks.x: Palette & fabrics

items: Palette: white, black, grey, navy, beige, olive, sage. Monochrome (shades of one neutral) reads expensive and intentional.
Fabrics for heat: cotton, linen, linen-blends. Avoid polyester — traps heat, looks cheap. Lighter colours in peak heat.
The camp-collar shirt is the hot-weather hero — reads stylish without heat.

blocks.x: The copy-this method

steps: Make a Pinterest board
Search 'men minimal outfit / smart casual men / neutral menswear'
Save 8–10 you like
Copy the pattern — you're curating, not inventing

blocks.x: Starter list: 3–4 heavyweight tees · 1 camp-collar shirt · 1 linen/cotton shirt · 1 relaxed straight trouser + 1 chino + 1 dark jean · 1 pair clean minimal sneakers · (later) 1 silver chain + a cap. Get everything tailored — it matters more than the brand.
Small surface area, outsized signal — the 'tells'. (Glasses live in Grooming → Glasses.)
Shoes

items: One clean, versatile pair kept spotless — your sneakers work as the default.
Shoes are where people check whether you 'tried' — scuffed or dirty undoes a good outfit.
Minimum: one pair that's clean and goes with your outfit formulas.

blocks.x: Fragrance

items: One good signature scent, applied lightly (2–3 sprays, not a cloud).
Invisible but unforgettable — smell is wired straight to memory and attraction.
Minimum: one versatile, fresh scent you wear by default.

blocks.x: Watch

items: One simple, clean watch signals intention without trying hard.
Keep it minimal and matched to your palette; never stack loud accessories.
Optional but high-leverage — quietly elevates the plainest outfit.

blocks.x: First moves: lock 2 outfit formulas and find a tailor this week. Statement pieces come after the basics are solid.

topics.name: Fragrance
topics.blurb: An invisible accessory: the wheel, the pyramid, and a sample-first way to pick one scent per occasion.

blocks.x: Fragrance is the one accessory you wear before anyone sees your outfit — it arrives half a second early and lingers after you leave. The whole thing is only three ideas: a wheel of scent families (what a smell says), a pyramid of notes (how a smell moves over hours), and a sample-first loop for picking the right one per occasion. Learn those three and you can walk into any shop and not drown.

Picks in this topic are illustrative benchmarks, not gospel. Skin chemistry, climate and your own nose decide everything — the only real vote is a test on your own skin. Nothing here is medical or authoritative; treat every named bottle as “start here,” not “buy this.”
A. The Fragrance Wheel — what each family says

Every scent belongs to a family, and the families sit on a wheel. The modern wheel collapses into 4 super-families — Fresh, Floral, Amber, Woody — each split into sub-families. The geometry is the whole point: neighbours blend smoothly (citrus flows into green, amber into woods) while opposite families contrast (Fresh sits across from Amber — light/cool vs warm/heavy). Note the modern rename: what older charts called “Oriental” is now “Amber.”

blocks.cap: The 4 super-families and their sub-families. Neighbours on the wheel blend; opposites contrast.

blocks.x: FRESH — clean, energetic, daytime & office. Citrus: zesty, clean, energetic. Aromatic / Fougère: the classic “barbershop” masculine — lavender + herbs, reads professional. Green: crisp, natural, sharp (cut grass, stems). Water / Aquatic: airy, clean, marine — the safest office scent.

FLORAL — romantic, soft, elegant. Floral: romantic, feminine-leaning but genuinely unisex. Soft Floral: powdery, elegant, refined. Fruity: playful, youthful, sweet-tart. Floral-Fruity: flirty, easy to like.

AMBER (formerly Oriental) — warm, sensual, spicy; evening & cold weather. Amber: warm, resinous, spicy. Soft Amber: cozy, sweet, gourmand-adjacent (vanilla, tonka). Woody Amber: warm + woody, sophisticated.
WOODY — grounding, confident, sophisticated. Woods: smooth cedar / sandalwood, refined. Mossy Woods / Chypre: sophisticated, vintage, built on oakmoss. Dry Woods: smoky, leathery, rugged.

args.title: The core axis of the wheel: cool & airy on the left, warm & heavy on the right.

blocks.cap: Fresh ↔ Amber — the single most useful axis. Day/office lives left; date/winter lives right.

items.x: Reads professional, approachable, low-risk. Default for office, meetings, hot weather.
Reads romantic and soft. Great for dates and social settings; unisex when balanced with wood.
Reads intimate and confident. Evenings, cold weather, when you want to be remembered.
Reads mature, put-together, quietly powerful. The safest way to smell expensive.

blocks.x: B. The Fragrance Pyramid — a moving picture, not a snapshot

A scent is not one smell; it is a timeline. Perfumers stack notes by weight so the fragrance evolves on your skin over hours. You are smelling a moving picture, not a photograph — which is why the first two minutes tell you almost nothing.

blocks.cap: Light molecules flash off first; heavy ones anchor the drydown. Read a scent bottom-up for what it really is.

items.x: First 5–15 min, most volatile. Citrus & light fruits & aromatic herbs: bergamot, lemon, lavender, mint. What sells you in the store — and what disappears fastest.
~20–60 min, the real personality. Florals, spices, fruits: rose, jasmine, cinnamon, geranium. This is what you actually 'wear' for most of the day.
6+ hours, heaviest molecules + fixatives that slow evaporation: musk, amber, vanilla, sandalwood, cedar, oakmoss, leather. This is what people smell when they hug you goodbye.

blocks.x: Raw material → layer, by weight. Light & volatile (citrus, herbs) → top. Medium-weight florals & spices → heart. Heavy resins, woods, musks & fixatives → base. Same logic as the wheel axis: light on top, heavy at the bottom.
C. How notes & families interact — accords & the arc

Blend two or more notes and you get an accord — a new smell that is more than its parts (like a chord, not single notes). Perfumers stack top→heart→base and lean on the wheel: neighbouring families blend harmoniously, while counterpart families create deliberate tension. A classic arc opens citrus-fresh, warms through florals, and lands on woody-amber — a bright hello that dries down to a warm hug.

blocks.cap: A sample accord arc: fresh opening → floral character → warm woody-amber drydown.

blocks.x: What a blend 'represents' is the story it tells over time and the vibe it projects: a fresh-to-warm arc says “approachable but not forgettable.” A pure-fresh scent says “clean, no drama.” A pure-amber says “here for the evening.” Choosing a fragrance is really choosing which arc you want people to experience.
D. Family field guide — vibe, occasion, benchmark, affordable

For each main family: the vibe, what it smells like, best occasions, a well-known designer benchmark (a widely-recognised reference), and an affordable route (clone / budget house). Use benchmarks to learn the category, then test the cheaper option on your own skin.

src: Benchmark: Acqua di Parma Colonia / Dior Sauvage (top)
Affordable: Zara Vibrant Leather / basic cologne
def: Fades fast — low longevity; best as a fresh burst

src: Affordable: Armaf / Zara clones of Boss
def: Can read 'dated' if too old-school — pick a modern take

fn: Vibe: airy, clean, marine — the safe office pick
Occasions: office, hot climate, meeting strangers
src: Benchmark: Bleu de Chanel / Dior Sauvage
Affordable: Armaf Club de Nuit / Zara clones
def: Very common — safe but not distinctive

fn: Vibe: crisp, natural, sharp (cut grass, stems)
def: Sharpness can feel cold in winter
Skews feminine if very sweet — look for a woody base
Powder can read mature/old-fashioned to some
Can read too young/synthetic if overdone

fn: Occasions: date night, winter, going out
src: Benchmark: YSL La Nuit de L'Homme
def: Heavy — overspray in heat is a common mistake
Sweetness can cloy — one or two sprays only

src: Benchmark: Bleu de Chanel / Terre d'Hermès
def: Very safe — rarely a wrong choice, rarely daring
Strong personality — not an office default
Reformulated oakmoss varies — vintage vibe not for everyone

blocks.x: E. Concentrations — EDT vs EDP vs Parfum
The same juice at a higher oil percentage smells stronger, lasts longer, and sits closer to the skin. More oil is not 'better' — it changes the job the scent can do.

head: Type
Oil %
Longevity
Best for
rows: A quick fresh burst, very hot days
Most intense & intimate — evenings, one spray

blocks.x: Rule of thumb: EDT for day/heat, EDP as your all-rounder, Parfum for close-up evenings. Stronger = fewer sprays, not more.
F. The decision loop — how to actually choose one
Don't buy blind and don't buy 'a signature scent' on day one. Run this loop; it turns an overwhelming wall of bottles into 3–4 cheap samples and one calm decision per occasion.

blocks.cap: The fragrance decision loop: understand → shortlist → sample → skin-test → live → wear out → commit → grow.

steps: Understand the FAMILIES — decide the vibe you want (clean / romantic / warm / grounded)
Understand the PYRAMID & mixing logic — know it evolves; ignore the first 2 minutes
Shortlist by OCCASION — day/office, date/evening, casual, formal — 2–3 benchmark candidates each
Get SAMPLES / DECANTS — about 3–4 total, never full bottles yet
Test ON SKIN — one scent per wrist, spray and DON'T rub
LIVE with it a full day — follow top → heart → base
Wear it OUT — note sillage, projection, longevity, compliments, and how YOU feel
COMMIT to one per key occasion — then grow your collection slowly around it

blocks.x: G. Buying tips — beating overwhelm & the review trap
A fragrance counter is engineered to overwhelm your nose. Go in with a plan and you keep control.

items.x: Know your vibe + occasion before you walk in. Aimless sniffing = nose fatigue and a panic buy.
Your nose fatigues fast. Reset by smelling your own skin or coffee beans, then stop for the day.
Paper strips skip skin chemistry. Spray on skin (pulse points) for the real result.
You're smelling alcohol + top notes. Wait for the drydown — that's what you'll actually wear.
A decant (small vial) lets you live with it for days for a few dollars. Always cheaper than a mistake bottle.
Heat amplifies AND shortens a scent — go lighter (EDT, fewer sprays) in hot/humid weather.

blocks.x: The YouTube-review trap. A reviewer's nose, skin chemistry and climate are not yours. Use reviews to BUILD a shortlist, never to DECIDE. Your own skin test is the only real vote. Ignore hype; blind-buy only when it's cheap enough that a miss doesn't hurt.
Vocabulary you'll actually use

items.what: The scent trail you leave behind as you move — how far it travels.
sym: People notice you enter/leave a room
fix: Fewer sprays or an EDT to dial it down

items.what: How far the scent radiates from your skin right now (the 'bubble' around you).
why: Driven by oil % and note choice
sym: Others smell it at arm's length
fix: 1–2 sprays for close settings; more for outdoors

items.what: How many hours it lasts on your skin before fading.
why: Dry skin holds scent less well
sym: Scent gone by lunch = low longevity

items.what: Warm spots (wrists, neck, chest) amplify scent; unscented lotion first helps it grip. Layering = pairing a simple base (e.g. vanilla) under a scent.
sym: Scent lasts longer on moisturised skin
fix: Spray pulse points; don't rub wrists together (it crushes top notes)

items.what: A small vial of a fragrance, sold cheaply so you can test or travel without a full bottle.
why: Cheap way to trial the drydown over days
fix: Use decants as your whole shortlisting step

topics.name: Watches
topics.blurb: Types → movement → sizing → materials → complications → one benchmark + one affordable pick, then a fit-first decision.

blocks.x: A watch is jewellery that happens to tell time — it signals taste and how you spend money. The mental model is short: pick a type for the vibe, a movement for how you relate to it, a size that fits your wrist, and the right materials/complications. One good watch beats five random ones.
Picks below are illustrative benchmarks, not recommendations. Try on before buying — wrist size and taste decide everything.
A. Types — the vibe of each

blocks.cap: The main watch families: dress vs tool vs travel vs sport vs everyday.

items.x: Thin, plain dial, leather strap. Slides under a cuff. For suits, formal events. Vibe: elegant, understated.
Rotating bezel, chunky, water-resistant, luminous. Casual-to-smart. Vibe: rugged, versatile — the most popular all-rounder.
Simple legible dial, tough, often small. Casual/outdoor. Vibe: no-nonsense, utilitarian.
Large, high-legibility dial, often big numerals. Casual. Vibe: bold, adventurous.
Second time-zone hand + 24h bezel. Vibe: worldly, practical for travellers.
Built-in stopwatch (sub-dials + pushers). Sporty-smart. Vibe: busy, technical, energetic.
Clean do-it-all that flexes casual to smart. Vibe: reliable, low-drama — the safe first watch.

args.title: Where each type sits on casual ↔ formal.

blocks.cap: Casual ↔ formal: tool watches skew casual, dress watches anchor the formal end.

blocks.x: B. Movements — how the watch runs

items.x: Battery + quartz crystal. Accurate, cheap, near-zero maintenance. Best if you want 'set and forget'.
Self-winds from wrist motion; no battery. Craftsmanship, an heirloom, a sweeping second hand. Needs occasional service.
Quartz accuracy, charged by light or motion — no battery swaps. A practical middle ground.

blocks.x: C. Sizing — fit the wrist, not the trend

A watch that overhangs your wrist looks wrong no matter how nice it is. Three numbers matter: case diameter (face width), lug-to-lug (tip-to-tip — must not overhang your wrist), and thickness (thin slides under cuffs; thick reads sporty).
D. Materials & straps

items.x: Durable, versatile, flexes casual-to-smart. The default bracelet/case material.
Dressier, warmer. Best for dress and smart-casual; avoid heavy water.
Casual, cheap, swappable, tough. Instantly makes a watch sporty and relaxed.

blocks.x: E. Complications
A complication is any function beyond telling time. Keep it to what you'll use.

items: Date — the one everyone actually uses
GMT — a second time zone for travel
Chronograph — a stopwatch; sporty look even if unused
Day/date, moonphase, power reserve — nice but add cost & clutter

blocks.x: F. Benchmark + affordable, per type

src: Benchmark: a slim automatic dress watch
def: Too big or busy ruins the formal look

src: Benchmark: Seiko diver / Rolex Submariner as the icon
def: Very common look
Plain — not a statement piece
Big — check lug-to-lug

src: Benchmark: Rolex GMT-Master as the icon
def: Slightly thicker cases

src: Benchmark: Omega Speedmaster as the icon
def: Busy dial; often thicker
Jack of all trades by design

blocks.x: G. Decision framework

blocks.cap: Watch decision flow: use → size → movement → budget → try on.

steps: Define your USE & occasions (one everyday? a dress piece? a travel GMT?)
SIZE it to your wrist (case + lug-to-lug + thickness) — fit beats trend
Pick a MOVEMENT (quartz for fuss-free, automatic for craft, solar for practical)
Set a BUDGET TIER and shop within it — don't stretch for a trophy first watch
TRY IT ON before buying — photos lie about size and wrist presence

topics.name: Shoes
topics.blurb: Types → formality → construction → materials → colour → fit → care → a fit-first starter set that covers 80% of occasions.

blocks.x: Shoes are the quiet tell. People clock them last but judge them hardest — a scuffed pair undoes a good outfit, and a well-kept pair makes a cheap one look intentional. The mental model is short: pick a type for the vibe, read the formality, check construction for how long it lasts, get the colour and fit right, then keep it alive with a little care. A few versatile pairs beat a closet of one-trick shoes.
Picks below are illustrative benchmarks, not recommendations. Fit varies by brand and last, so always try on — the right size and shape decide everything.
A. Types — what each one signals

blocks.cap: The main shoe families. (*Monk-strap uses buckles, not laces — grouped with dress lace-ups for vibe.)

items.x: Closed lacing (the eyelet flaps sit under the vamp). The most formal shoe. Vibe: sharp, dressy — the suit-and-formal default.
Open lacing (flaps sit on top). Smart but versatile, roomier fit. Vibe: put-together yet easygoing — the safest smart shoe.
Slip-on, no laces. Smart-casual and summer-friendly. Vibe: relaxed polish, a touch continental.
Buckle(s) instead of laces. Distinctive, business-fashion. Vibe: stylish, deliberate — a step off the beaten path.
Decorative perforations (broguing). Less formal the more broguing it has. Vibe: characterful, textured — dresses an outfit down a notch.
Ankle boot with elastic side panels, no laces. Smart-casual, very versatile. Vibe: sleek, modern, effortless.
Ankle boot, 2–3 eyelets, often suede + crepe sole. Smart-casual, relaxed. Vibe: warm, understated, easy.
Clean lines, low profile, plain white/neutral leather. Smart-casual. Vibe: modern, tidy — dresses up jeans and chinos.
Runners / trainers with visible tech soles. Casual only. Vibe: sporty, off-duty.
Soft slip-on with rubber-nub soles. Casual. Vibe: laid-back, weekend, warm-weather.

blocks.x: B. Formality spectrum — casual to formal

Formality isn't just the shape — colour and material shift it too. As a rule: a black Oxford is the most formal shoe you can own, while suede and lighter colours read more casual no matter the style. The same silhouette dresses down in tan suede and up in black calf.

args.title: Where each type sits on casual ↔ formal (black + smooth leather pushes right; suede + colour pulls left).

blocks.cap: Casual ↔ formal: sneakers anchor casual; the black Oxford anchors the formal end.

blocks.x: C. Construction — why the sole matters
How the sole attaches to the upper decides how long a shoe lasts and whether it can be repaired. This is the difference between a shoe you own for a decade and one you bin in a year.

items.x: A leather strip (welt) stitched to upper + insole, then the sole stitched to the welt. Durable, water-resistant, and resoleable many times. Premium — the value play if you keep shoes.
Sole stitched directly through the insole. Sleeker, more flexible, lighter. Still resoleable (needs a specialist machine). Common on Italian dress shoes.
Sole glued on, no stitching. Cheap and light, but usually not resoleable — once the sole goes, the shoe goes. Fine for sneakers and budget pairs.

head: Construction
Durability
Resoleable?
Why you'd pick it
rows: Longevity & value on shoes you'll keep
Cheapest; fine for sneakers & occasional wear

blocks.x: Cost-per-wear beats sticker price. A resoleable welted shoe worn for years is often cheaper per wear than replacing glued shoes annually — and it ages better.
D. Materials — leather grades & suede
Leather quality runs on a ladder. Higher grades keep the natural surface, age gracefully, and take a shine; lower grades are coated and crack sooner.

head: Grade
What it is
Ages like
rows: Best — develops a patina, most durable
Good — smoother, a bit less durable
Poorly — can crack & peel; budget tier

items.x: Fine-grained, smooth calfskin — the dress-shoe standard. Takes a high polish; smart and formal.
Napped underside of the hide. Casual, soft, and stylish — but weather-sensitive. Needs a protector spray and a suede brush, not polish.
Smooth leather: condition + polish. Suede: brush + protect. Never polish suede or brush polish into smooth leather scratches.

blocks.x: E. Colour priority & matching

If you buy shoes in order, buy dark brown first — it's the most versatile and pairs with the widest range of outfits. Black second for formal needs. The classic rule still holds: match your shoe colour to your belt.

items.x: The workhorse. Pairs with navy, grey, and earth tones; works smart-casual to business. If you own one pair, make it this.
For formal and business-formal. Pairs with charcoal, grey, and black. A black Oxford covers the dressiest events.
Shoe colour = belt colour. Brown shoes → brown belt; black shoes → black belt. Metals (buckle, watch) ideally agree too.

blocks.x: F. Fit & last

A great shoe in the wrong size looks and feels wrong. Three things decide fit: length (a thumb's width of room at the toe), width (snug, no bulging or pinching), and heel slip (a little is normal in new leather; a lot means it's too big). The last — the foot-shaped mould the shoe is built on — sets both the fit and the vibe.

items.x: Roughly a thumb's width of space ahead of your longest toe. Too long looks clownish; too short cramps and creases badly.
Snug across the ball of the foot, no pinching. A slight heel slip in new leather settles as it breaks in; heavy slip = size down.
A round toe reads classic and relaxed; an almond or chiselled toe reads sleeker and dressier. Same shoe, different vibe.

blocks.x: Sizing varies by brand and last. Your number in one maker isn't your number in another — always try the specific model, ideally late in the day when feet are largest.
G. Care & rotation
This is where return-per-effort is highest: a few small habits double a shoe's life and keep it looking new. Care is cheaper than replacement.

blocks.cap: The care loop: rotate and rest, tree, clean, condition, protect, and resole welted shoes to keep them going for years.

items: Shoe trees (cedar): insert after wearing — they hold shape and absorb moisture and odour.
Rotate pairs: never wear the same shoes two days running; let them rest ~24h to dry.
Smooth leather: brush off dirt, condition occasionally, then polish for shine and protection.
Suede: brush with a suede brush, use a protector spray, and never apply polish.
Resole welted shoes before the sole wears into the upper — it's a fraction of the price of new.

blocks.x: H. Occasion matching
Match the shoe to the setting. When in doubt, a dark-brown Derby covers the widest middle ground.

head: Occasion
Best types
Colour
rows: Derby, Loafer, Chelsea, Chukka, minimal sneaker

blocks.x: I. Benchmark + affordable, per type
For each key type: a widely-recognised benchmark to learn the category, and an affordable route. Use benchmarks to understand what 'good' looks like, then try the cheaper option on your own feet.

src: Benchmark: a welted Oxford like Loake / Meermin
Affordable: Clarks or a local welted maker
def: Needs breaking in; keep polished
Can be too casual for formal suits

src: Benchmark: a quality leather Chelsea boot
def: Suede versions need weather care
Keep them spotless or the effect is lost

src: Affordable: any reputable trainer on sale
def: Casual only — don't dress it up

blocks.x: Picks are examples to illustrate 'benchmark vs affordable', not endorsements. Fit, comfort, and your own taste on your own feet are the deciding vote.
J. Decision framework (80/20)

You don't need many shoes — you need the right few. A dark-brown Derby or Oxford, a clean minimal white sneaker, and a Chelsea or chukka boot together cover roughly 80% of occasions. Buy fit-first, prioritise construction for the pairs you'll keep for years, and only then expand into specialised styles.

blocks.cap: Shoe decision flow: occasions → fit → construction → the versatile starter three → expand.

steps: Define the OCCASIONS you actually dress for (business? smart-casual? mostly casual?)
Buy FIT-FIRST — length, width, heel slip, and a last shape that suits you; sizing varies by brand
Prioritise CONSTRUCTION (Goodyear welt / Blake) for shoes you'll keep and resole
Get the STARTER THREE: a dark-brown Derby or Oxford, a clean white minimal sneaker, a Chelsea/chukka boot
CARE for them — trees, rotation, brush/condition/polish — to stretch their life
EXPAND slowly — add loafers, monks, or colours only as real occasions demand

tabs.name: Photos / Socials
tabs.intro: A tactical weekend project: pose first, then profile, then image.

blocks.x: Your third real gap, and a tactical one. The order matters: learn to pose first, then build the profile, then shape the overall image. A weekend project, not years.

Most guys blame the camera or the lighting. The real variable is how you carry yourself in front of it. The same face looks completely different relaxed-and-angled vs. stiff-and-square. Learn a few poses and every photo after improves automatically.
Posing → Profile → Image

blocks.cap: Three steps, in order — start with posing, not gear.

topics.name: Posing
topics.blurb: Stand like it's no big deal.

blocks.x: Learn to stand in front of a camera like it's no big deal.

items: Don't face the lens square — angle your body slightly, weight on the back foot.
Relax the shoulders down; push the chin slightly forward and down (kills double-chin, defines jaw).
Give your hands a job — in a pocket, adjusting a sleeve, holding something. Idle hands look awkward.
Slightly candid beats stiff-posed — look away, half-laugh, walk and shoot. Find your better side and practice in a mirror.

blocks.x: A clean, lived-in profile — not a content grind.

items: A simple, real bio and a clear profile photo. No try-hard.
A grid that looks like a life: a few good photos across hobbies, travel, friends, the gym/swim.
Post occasionally, quality over quantity; use stories for the casual day-to-day.
Minimum: a profile that makes someone think 'this guy's interesting,' not 'this is empty.'

blocks.x: The handful of photos that answer the silent questions.
Every viewer subconsciously asks: what do you look like, what's your life like, would hanging out be fun? Answer each once.

items.x: What you look like.
Shows your build + fit.
What your life's like.
Would hanging out be fun.

items: Light is everything — natural daylight beats any filter. Get a friend to shoot a golden-hour set in one session.
Avoid: bathroom mirror selfies, group-only photos, heavy filters, sunglasses in every pic.

blocks.x: First move: 20 minutes practicing poses in a mirror, then one golden-hour photo walk with a friend — that's your whole set.

tabs.name: Communities
tabs.intro: The engine that creates the encounters. Confidence is built; opportunity is engineered.

blocks.x: Everything else is useless if you're never in rooms with new people. 'Meeting someone' feels like luck, but luck is just how many quality interactions you're exposed to per week. You don't need to be smoother — you need to be in more rooms, more often, with the same people repeatedly.
Encounters = Rooms/week × People/room × Repetition

blocks.cap: Luck is a volume game with a multiplier called repetition.

blocks.x: Warm beats cold. A recurring group means you meet the same faces again and again — low pressure, no 'approach' needed, familiarity does the work. The cheat code while you're still building social comfort.
Two recurring communities you actually show up to.

items: Pick 2 recurring things: e.g. dance class + a run club, hobby group, sport, or meetup.
Bias toward mixed-gender, recurring settings over one-off events.
Dance class counts double — comfort on a dance floor and a community.
Minimum: two things on your weekly calendar where you'll see new people.

blocks.x: Consistency turns strangers into familiar faces.

items: Go often enough that people recognize you — that's when warmth starts for free.
Say yes to invitations and after-events; be a regular, not a tourist.
Learn names, ask follow-ups, be the easy-to-talk-to one (see Communication).
Minimum: enough repetition that you're 'one of the group', not a newcomer each time.

blocks.x: Overlapping circles that generate their own opportunities.

items: Connect people across your circles — being a connector is high status and makes you central.
Eventually host or organize — the strongest social position there is.
A point where introductions, events, and invites come to you.
This is the long game — the floor is just: be in 2 rooms, consistently.

blocks.x: First move: choose 2 recurring communities this month and treat attendance like the gym — non-negotiable reps.

tabs.name: Physique
tabs.intro: Not an aesthetic body — clothes fit you and you don't feel weak in your head.

blocks.x: The goal isn't an aesthetic body — it's that clothes fit you and you don't feel weak in your head. You're already at the minimum.

items: Gym 4–5×/week with compound lifts at the core; add weight/reps over time.
You're already doing this — keep it boring and consistent.
Minimum: clothes fill out at the shoulders and you don't read as frail. You're here.

blocks.x: Protein-forward, enough to build.

items: Hit a daily protein target; eat in a slight surplus to add muscle.
Hydrate, sleep — recovery is where the gains actually happen.
Minimum: eating with intent, not by accident.

blocks.x: How you stand changes how the body reads.

items: Stand tall, shoulders back and down, chest open, chin level.
Posture changes your silhouette more than a few kg ever will — and signals confidence directly.
Minimum: catch yourself slouching and reset. Free, immediate, high-impact.

blocks.x: Maintenance: gym 4–5×/week, protein + sleep. Runs on autopilot once it's habit.

topics.name: Body Analysis
topics.blurb: A full read of your body — in diagrams.

blocks.x: A complete read of your body across the three angles. Three things to understand: your type (where your composition sits today), your structure (frame, muscle, fat), and the plan that follows from it. The headline is encouraging — everything limiting you here is the changeable kind.

items.x: Not overweight (BMI ~23). The 'skinny-fat' pattern: lean limbs, low muscle, some central fat.
Medium-narrow frame, shallow V-taper. Shoulders and central fat are the two levers.
Build muscle + shed central fat at once. Redistribution, not weight loss.

blocks.x: 01 · Body type — where you sit today

blocks.cap: Low muscle with some central fat = the 'skinny-fat' quadrant. The goal isn't smaller — it's up and slightly left: more muscle, less central fat.

blocks.x: At ~5'9" and ~71 kg your BMI is around 23 — normal. So this isn't a weight problem, it's a composition and distribution one, and that's the version that responds fastest to the right training.
02 · Frame & proportions

blocks.cap: Medium-to-narrow frame with a shallow shoulder-to-waist taper. The width you see at the middle is the belly sitting forward, not the waist spreading sideways.

blocks.x: Both things limiting your proportions are the fixable ones: shoulder width is buildable, and forward-sitting central fat shrinks quickly. Wider delts also make the waist look smaller by contrast — free value.
03 · Muscle map — what to build first

blocks.cap: Priority order for your frame: shoulders first, then upper back and chest for the V-taper, arms and core alongside, legs as a maintained base.

blocks.x: Muscle mass is currently low across the board — which is simply an empty canvas, not a flaw. Shoulders are the single highest-leverage build for how your whole upper body reads.
04 · Fat distribution

blocks.cap: Fat is centrally distributed — lower belly, sitting forward — while the limbs stay lean. This is normal-weight central adiposity: the most responsive fat type to change.

items: Oiliness of the plan: add muscle and clean up inputs, and the belly is where visible change shows up.
Central fat responds most to overall activity, sleep, stress and diet quality — not just calorie cuts.
You do not need to get smaller — you need to redistribute.

blocks.x: 05 · Posture — the free upgrade

blocks.cap: A mild forward-head / rounded-upper-back tendency, plus a slight anterior pelvic tilt that arches the low back and pushes the belly further forward than the fat alone would.

blocks.x: This is genuinely good news: some of the belly prominence is postural, so better core control and hip/hamstring work flattens the look before you've lost much fat at all.
06 · The plan — body recomposition

blocks.cap: Recomposition: build muscle and reduce central fat at the same time. Four levers — training, protein, core/mobility, and the sleep/steps/stress multipliers.

items: Progressive resistance training biased to shoulders, upper back and legs — getting stronger over months is the lever. (You're already in the gym 4–5×/week — see Training.)
Protein-forward eating — roughly 1.6–2 g per kg bodyweight to actually build muscle. This is muscle-gain territory, not crash-dieting.
Core + hip mobility — anti-extension core (planks, dead-bugs) and hip/hamstring work to address the pelvic tilt.
Sleep, daily steps, stress — the boring multipliers that specifically influence central fat.

blocks.x: The honest summary: a lean frame carrying low muscle and forward-sitting belly fat, with a little postural exaggeration on top — and every one of those is among the most changeable things a body can present with. This is a strong place to build from, and you're already running the engine.

A note: this is fitness guidance, not medical or nutritional prescription. For a plan dialled to your numbers, a coach or registered dietitian can tailor it; if central fat is a health concern, a doctor can check the basics. Treat this as a map.

tabs.name: Communication
tabs.intro: The highest-leverage area. At MVP stage you don't need wit — you need to not freeze.

blocks.x: The highest-leverage area of all. At the MVP stage you don't need wit or charisma — you need to not freeze.

The bar isn't 'be impressive / funny / smooth.' It's be genuinely interested and easy to talk to. People remember how you made them feel, not your best line. Move the pressure off 'how am I doing?' and onto 'who is this person?' — that single shift kills most anxiety.
Open → Sustain → Flirt → Exit + the ask

blocks.cap: The arc of any interaction, online or offline.

blocks.x: Reps beat theory: talk to low-stakes strangers daily (baristas, gym, shops) with no goal but practice. Volume removes the fear faster than anything else.
The texting arc: open on something specific, keep it moving, add warmth, then move it off the app.
Opening

items: Open on something specific in their profile or photos — never just 'hey'.
Ask one easy, open question that's effortless to answer.
Keep it short and light; send once, then let them reply.

blocks.x: Sustaining

items: Ask follow-ups and share a bit of yourself — don't interrogate.
Match their energy and message length; mirror the vibe.
Move it forward before it fizzles — a voice note or a plan beats endless texting.

blocks.x: Flirting

items: Light teasing, a specific genuine compliment, a little cheeky banter.
Read their replies and calibrate — match, don't overwhelm.
Easy on the emojis; confidence reads as relaxed, not eager.

blocks.x: Exit & the ask

items: Once there's rapport, suggest a low-key first meet — coffee or a walk.
Be specific with a day; vague plans die.
Minimum: you move promising chats to a real meeting, not pen-pal limbo.

blocks.x: In person, the same arc — warmth and interest carry you far before any technique.
Opening

items: Lead with an observation or light question ('this place is packed' / 'how do you know everyone here?').
It doesn't need to be clever — it needs to be warm and easy.
Minimum: one go-to opener you can use anywhere without overthinking.
Reps: talk to low-stakes strangers daily with no goal but practice.

blocks.x: Sustaining

items: Follow-up-question habit: turn every answer into the next question, one layer deeper.
Actually listen and react — then share a bit of yourself so it's a conversation, not an interview.
Relaxed body language, eye contact, unhurried pace.

blocks.x: Flirting

items: Light teasing, holding eye contact a beat longer, a genuine compliment.
It's calibration, not a script — read their energy and match it.
More advanced; warmth + interest already carries you far.

blocks.x: Exit & the ask

items: Exit before it fizzles: 'I'm gonna grab a drink — this was fun, what's your Insta?'
Asking for a contact is normal and low-stakes; treat it that way and it stays light.
Minimum: you can end an interaction cleanly and get a way to follow up.

blocks.x: The slow, compounding game of being funny — parked for now, deliberately.

items: Notice what makes banter land; keep the lines and rhythms that work.
Timing, storytelling, callbacks, observational humor — all learnable. Study what lands (good standup, sharp sitcoms) and notice why.
Lean playful, never try-hard — forced jokes read worse than none.
Parked (Excluded, Low) — warmth + interest is enough at the MVP stage.

tabs.name: Cult
tabs.intro: Culture & interests — conversation fuel and the venues where you meet people.

blocks.x: References are conversation fuel and enjoyment — not a prerequisite. You've watched tons of films; you're past the minimum. Don't let an unfinished sitcom become a reason to wait.
Film & TV — your strength

items: Loads of films watched — you can hold opinions and follow references.
Sitcoms (Friends, HIMYM, Big Bang) add shared humor + references; enjoyable, not required.
Minimum: cleared. Anime and deeper film knowledge are extras.

blocks.x: Books — talking points

items: You're reading — keep a couple of current books you can mention naturally.
Ideas from books are great conversation fuel without trying hard.
Minimum: one or two things you're reading you can speak about with energy.

blocks.x: Music — quiet signal

items: Have a sense of what you like and why — music comes up constantly.
Useful for events, playlists, shared discovery with someone new.
Minimum: you can answer 'what do you listen to?' with something real.

blocks.x: Consume this for pleasure, in the background. It compounds naturally — no sprint needed.
Hobbies do double duty: things to talk about with real energy, and the places you actually meet people. Fuel and venue, not a gate.

blocks.cap: The best interests do all three at once.

blocks.x: What you have

items: Gym, learning to swim, reading, films — that's a real set.
At least one (gym/swim) puts you around other people.
Minimum: cleared. You're not short on things to talk about.

blocks.x: What to build

items: Learning to swim right now — conditioning plus a genuine skill. Keep at it.
Dance — pull this earlier than 'distant future'; it dissolves club self-consciousness.
Music (an instrument), advanced swimming, a sport — hobbies deep enough to be interesting and to meet people through.

blocks.x: Using them socially

items: Each interest is both a story to tell and a room full of potential people.
Pick at least one that's group-based (links straight to Communities).
Minimum: your interests pull you toward people, not away from them.

blocks.x: Dance class is the high-value pull here — comfort and community in one.
